title: Biblical, Not Secular
orientation: A tenet page from Abolitionists Rising arguing that opposition to abortion must be grounded explicitly in Scripture rather than secular pragmatism, addressed to Christians and church leaders weighing whether to back abolitionism over pro-life incrementalism.
audiences: Christians weighing abolitionism vs. the pro-life movement
Pastors and church leaders considering their congregation's duty
Pro-life activists open to a biblical critique of incrementalism
New abolitionists learning the movement's theological grounding
Skeptics who view abortion as a purely secular policy issue

questions.question: What makes abolitionism different from just being against abortion?
questions.audience: New abolitionists learning the movement's theological grounding
questions.answer: Abolitionism is framed as more than ending a practice; it is bringing the truth of God's word into direct conflict with the evils of the age. The article contrasts pro-life arguments based on pain or heartbeats with the abolitionist claim that abortion is wrong because humans are made in God's image and Christ became man to rescue mankind.
questions.quote: To be an abolitionist is more than simply working to end something. Anyone can work to end something. Abolitionism is about bringing the truth revealed in God’s word into conflict with the evils of the age and the worldly wisdom of man.

questions.question: Why do abolitionists refuse to negotiate gestational limits like 24 vs 28 weeks?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists open to a biblical critique of incrementalism
questions.answer: The article rejects testing public opinion to set incremental gestational thresholds, calling that a worldly tactic. Instead, abolitionists are to proclaim that abortion is murder and that justice requires immediate abolition, leaving outcomes to God.
questions.quote: That means we don’t do things like test the winds to see whether the people support moving from a ban of abortion at 28 weeks to a ban at 24 weeks at this particular moment in time. We simply proclaim the truth that abortion is murder and that justice demands it be abolished immediately.

questions.question: What scripture do abolitionists cite against pro-life rape and incest exceptions?
questions.audience: Christians weighing abolitionism vs. the pro-life movement
questions.answer: The article appeals to Proverbs 24, Isaiah 10, and Romans 3:8 to argue that incremental laws permitting abortion in some cases are 'iniquitous decrees' and amount to doing evil that good may come. It singles out children conceived in rape as the most fatherless, whom such laws make prey.
questions.quote: As abolitionists who take God's word seriously, we never write laws which make the fatherless prey and there's no one more fatherless than the child conceived in rape. The pro-life movement has long been content to write laws allowing for the murder of the most fatherless among us because of the crimes of their fathers.

questions.question: Why is Moses called the first abolitionist?
questions.audience: New abolitionists learning the movement's theological grounding
questions.answer: The article casts Moses as a prototypical abolitionist because he refused every partial deal Pharaoh offered to free only some Hebrews, for a limited time, or without their livestock. His 'not a hoof shall be left behind' is presented as the model for refusing compromise on liberation.
questions.quote: Pharaoh offered all sorts of deals to Moses. He offered to let some of the Hebrews go, he offered to let the Hebrews go for a certain amount of time, and he even offered to let all the Hebrews go free permanently so long as they left their animals – to which Moses replied, “Not a hoof shall be left behind” (Exodus 10:24-26).

questions.question: How should Christians respond when courts protect abortion?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders considering their congregation's duty
questions.answer: Citing Acts 5:29, the article argues that when civil authorities like the Supreme Court contradict God, Christians must obey God rather than man. The proper response is to boldly bring the Gospel into conflict with the court's rebellion against God.
questions.quote: So when the supreme court orders our state to allow the mass murder of preborn children, we boldly bring the Gospel into conflict with the court's rebellion against God.

questions.question: What does the article say my actual duty is as a Christian beyond donating?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders considering their congregation's duty
questions.answer: The article says donating is not enough. Christians are exhorted to preach at abortion facilities, meet with state and local officials, support pregnant single mothers, share abolitionist content, and recruit fellow believers, while still acknowledging that financial support of abolitionist projects is also appropriate.
questions.quote: Go to your local killing center and preach the Gospel to those stumbling toward slaughter. Schedule a meeting with your state senators, state representatives, state executives, city councils, and any other magistrates to explain to them their duty before God and Constitution to immediately abolish abortion. Spiritually and materially aid the pregnant single mothers in your neighborhood or church.

questions.question: Why ground the case against abortion in religion instead of secular arguments?
questions.audience: Skeptics who view abortion as a purely secular policy issue
questions.answer: The article quotes William Lloyd Garrison to insist that successful human rights movements have historically rested on bringing the Gospel against the evil of their day. It treats secular framing as 'worldly wisdom' insufficient to confront child sacrifice.
questions.quote: Perhaps the best articulation of what it means to bring the Gospel into conflict with the evil of the age comes from slavery abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison when he said, “My fanaticism is to make Christianity the enemy of all that is sinful.”

questions.question: Whose responsibility are the results of abolitionist work?
questions.audience: New abolitionists learning the movement's theological grounding
questions.answer: The article frames the abolitionist's job as faithful proclamation, not strategic outcome management. Christians are to declare God's word to people and magistrates and trust God with the results.
questions.quote: The duty to make such proclamations is ours, and the results belong to God.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Companion tenet contrasting abolitionism with the pro-life movement critiqued here.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Develops the immediatist stance this article uses to reject incremental gestational bans.

related.title: No Exceptions
related.reason: Expands the rejection of rape and incest exceptions cited from Isaiah 10.

related.reason: Lays out the broader scriptural framework this tenet page summarizes.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Applies the same biblical reasoning to criminalizing abortion equally with other homicide.

related.reason: Case study answering a prominent pro-life leader with the same biblical-vs-secular argument.

related.reason: Cross-site theological attack on the incrementalism this article calls iniquitous.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Concise framing of the incrementalism vs. immediatism dispute referenced throughout.
